html:
Five suckings, or three, or ten — hadith fluidity on the breastfeeding threshold Women Logical Inconsistency Contradiction Moderate Abu Dawud #2062
"Does Breast-Feeding Less Than Five Times Establish Fosterage?" [chapter title]

[Classical sources preserve variants: five suckings, three, ten, one with satiation...]

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud preserves the debate over how many breastfeedings establish "foster-kinship" — the bond that prohibits marriage between parties. Different hadiths give different numbers: five, three, ten, or any single satiation feed.

Why this is a problem

A hadith from Aisha states the Quran originally contained a verse specifying ten breastfeedings, later abrogated and replaced by five — yet the "five" verse itself is not in the current Quran. This is another implicit admission of Quranic incompleteness. Meanwhile, whether two adults who were nursed by the same woman decades ago are legally prohibited from marrying depends on an accurate count that few families would reliably remember.

The tradition has made a marriage-invalidating rule whose core numerical value is contested in the foundational texts. Jurists chose among competing options; the choice is inherently arbitrary because the sources themselves do not settle it.

Why it fails

"Legitimate flexibility" does not resolve the problem when the rule has marriage-invalidating consequences. If the threshold is five and a family counted four, a marriage that should be prohibited proceeds; if it was three and a different school governs, the same facts produce opposite legal outcomes. A rule whose central value is undecidable by the tradition's own sources cannot be called a settled divine law.

Separating a mother slave from her child — permitted after age seven Prophetic Character Logical Inconsistency Moderate Abu Dawud Book 24, Ch. 52
[Chapter and hadiths discussing the prohibition on separating mothers from their children during slave sales.]

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud preserves rulings on when a mother slave may and may not be sold separately from her child. Muhammad disapproved of separation; classical rulings codified a partial prohibition — generally forbidding separation until the child reached weaning age (around seven), after which separation was permitted.

Why this is a problem

The existence of these rulings documents that mother-child slave separations were routine enough to require judicial guidance. The "protection" amounts to: do not sell an eight-year-old away from his mother until he is seven. A reform that permits selling an eight-year-old child to a different owner than his mother is a limited reform of an unlimited cruelty.

Islamic apologetics frequently describes the religion as working toward the abolition of slavery. The tradition's detailed regulations about how to conduct slave sales — including infant-separation rules — contradicts the framing that the institution was always on a path to reform.

Why it fails

Restricting the age at which children can be torn from their mothers is a regulation of cruelty, not its abolition. The core transaction — buying and selling human beings — was never questioned. A trajectory that regulates edge cases without challenging the institution's existence is a trajectory toward more humane slavery, not toward freedom.

Twelve leaders prophecy — Sunni and Shia both claim the same hadith Logical Inconsistency Contradiction Moderate Abu Dawud #4279
"This religion will continue to endure until there have been twelve Khalifah ruling over you, all of whom are agreed upon by the Ummah."

What the hadith says

A prophecy of twelve leaders for the Muslim community. Sunnis identify them with various caliphs; Shia identify them with the twelve Imams, the twelfth of whom is in occultation. Both traditions claim this hadith as validation.

Why this is a problem

The 1,400-year Sunni-Shia split has one of its anchors in how to identify these twelve. Neither side has a clean list: Sunnis cannot produce twelve caliphs agreed upon by the whole ummah; Shia have a clean twelve, but the Twelfth has been physically absent from history for over a millennium. A prophecy specific enough to generate the central schism of Islam while being too vague to resolve it is not functioning as prophecy.

The ambiguity is structural, not accidental. A clearer text — naming the twelve — would settle the matter. The vagueness allows both sectarian readings to co-exist; it is the pattern of interpretable prophecy, not specific prediction.

Why it fails

A prophecy whose "multiple valid interpretations" have produced over a millennium of sectarian warfare — including assassinations, battles, and ongoing political violence — has not been usefully prophetic. The claim that doctrinal conflict is "richness" papers over the body count the dispute has generated.

"Do not kill children" — a rule that reveals what needed to be forbidden Treatment of Disbelievers Logical Inconsistency Moderate Abu Dawud #2613
"Do not kill a frail old man, nor an infant, nor a young child, nor a woman. Do not steal from the spoils of war, and do not break your promises, and do not mutilate (the dead enemy) and do not kill children."

What the hadith says

Muhammad's instructions to fighters departing on campaign included prohibitions: do not kill old men, infants, children, or women; do not mutilate corpses; do not steal from the spoils.

Why this is a problem

A prohibition reveals what was otherwise expected. Muhammad had to specifically instruct his men not to kill children and elderly non-combatants, which documents that killing them was within the assumed range of conduct without explicit rebuke. The instruction establishes Muhammad as more humane than his baseline culture — and also establishes what the baseline was.

The companion hadith (Abu Dawud #2672) permits civilian deaths in night raids: "they are from them." Jurisprudence harmonizes the two by distinguishing deliberate targeting from incidental killing — but that distinction makes the prohibition effectively override-able in any militarily convenient situation.

Why it fails

Being ahead of a low bar is not a virtue to be celebrated without qualification. The parallel night-raid permission undercuts the non-combatant protection. A moral framework for war that needed to specifically prohibit killing infants — and then preserved a permission for killing civilians incidentally — has not eliminated the problem; it has managed it selectively.

Muhammad's exclusive intercession — every other prophet declines Jesus / Christology Logical Inconsistency Moderate Abu Dawud hadiths on intercession
[Standard intercession hadith:] "On the Day of Resurrection, people will seek Adam's intercession, then Noah's, then Abraham's, then Moses', then Jesus'. Each will say: 'I am not able. Go to another.' Finally they will come to Muhammad, and he will say: 'I am the one.'"

What the hadith says

On Judgment Day, humanity appeals in turn to Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus to intercede. Each declines, citing a past personal failing. Only Muhammad accepts. The failing assigned to Jesus varies by version; one narration cites his community taking him as God.

Why this is a problem

Jesus declining intercession directly contradicts Christian theology, which identifies Jesus as the Great Intercessor. The Islamic narrative has Jesus refusing the one role his tradition assigns him as its summit. The story also requires every prior prophet to be displayed as inadequate so that Muhammad can be displayed as uniquely adequate — a structure that serves its narrator's interest rather than independent theological analysis.

Muhammad himself is told in the Quran to seek forgiveness for his sins (47:19, 48:2). A prophet commanded to seek forgiveness is not obviously better qualified to intercede for others than prophets whose listed failings are minor.

Why it fails

The "humility" reading does not explain why the structure requires every prior prophet to fail before Muhammad succeeds. A narrative in which the last claimant wins after all predecessors are eliminated is a competitive ranking story, not a humility demonstration. The rhetorical purpose — elevating Muhammad above every prior prophet — is evident in the structure regardless of the theological gloss.

The death list at the conquest of Mecca — satirists marked for execution Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Moderate Abu Dawud #2683
"On the day of the conquest of Makkah, the Prophet gave protection to all people except four men and two women, whom he said should be killed even if they were found clinging to the coverings of the Ka'bah."

What the hadith says

At the conquest of Mecca, Muhammad declared a general amnesty with specific exceptions. Six individuals were marked for execution regardless of location — including within the sanctuary of the Ka'ba. Two were singing-girls who had composed satirical verses against him; others were former apostates or critics.

Why this is a problem

Two of the six were women who wrote satirical poetry about Muhammad. The penalty for satire was death. Modern claims that Islam has no blasphemy-death doctrine run directly into this precedent. The Ka'ba sanctuary — whose covering is normally a plea for protection — was explicitly waived for these individuals. Muhammad's amnesty is celebrated in Islamic tradition as an act of magnanimity; the list of exceptions is typically omitted from that celebration.

Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, Iran, and other jurisdictions have laws criminalizing insult to Muhammad, sometimes with capital sentences. The precedent for those laws is this list.

Why it fails

Two of the six were women condemned specifically for poetic mockery. The tradition's own sources identify their offense as satirical verse. A mercy that carves out a death list for satirists is a mercy whose edges define what cannot be forgiven — and the edges have structured Islamic blasphemy law ever since.

Every martyr gets 72 wide-eyed virgins in paradise Women Jesus / Christology Strange / Obscure Moderate Tirmidhi #1663
"Every martyr... will be married to seventy-two Hur al-'Ayn (wide-eyed virgins)..."

[Abu Dawud preserves the general framework; the specific number appears prominently in Tirmidhi and Ibn Majah.]

What the hadith says

Islamic martyrdom theology promises the male martyr a package of paradise rewards, prominently including 72 virgin maidens (houris) for his eternal sexual pleasure.

Why this is a problem

The reward is explicitly and specifically sexual. Classical commentaries describe the houris' physical features, their eternal virginity that renews after each encounter, and their function as pleasure-objects. This has been operationalized by modern extremist groups: Hamas, ISIS, and others have used the 72-virgin promise in direct recruitment propaganda. The reward structure targets young men, which is both its design and its evidence: female martyrs receive no parallel reward of 72 male counterparts.

A 2000 philological argument (Christopher Luxenberg) proposed that "houri" in Syriac originally meant "white raisins" — a consolation prize rather than virgins. The tradition rejects this reading, but the proposal itself indicates the textual basis is more fragile than it appears.

Why it fails

Classical commentary is not metaphorical: it specifies physical features, sexual function, and renewal mechanics. The extremist recruitment use of the specific number (72) is accurate, not a misreading. A paradise economy that specifies sexual inventory as the primary martyr reward has designed an incentive structure for violence in exactly the way the evidence shows it has functioned.

"They are from them" — Muhammad authorizes night raids with civilian deaths Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Moderate Abu Dawud #2672
"[The companions asked] about the settlements of the idolaters when they are under attack at night, and their children and women are killed. The Prophet said: 'They are from them.'"

What the hadith says

Asked specifically about night raids where women and children would be killed alongside fighters, Muhammad replied that the civilians shared the combatants' status — "they are from them." No instruction was given to spare them specifically.

Why this is a problem

Modern international humanitarian law prohibits collective punishment because civilians are not responsible for their community's decisions. This hadith endorses the opposite principle: family or tribal membership transfers legal combatant status to non-fighters. Night raids are inherently indiscriminate, and Muhammad's permission in this worst case means there was no scenario in which civilian protection was treated as paramount.

The tradition preserves both this permission and the separate prohibition on killing women and children (Abu Dawud #2613). Jurisprudence harmonizes them by distinguishing deliberate targeting from incidental killing — which makes the "they are from them" hadith effective cover for any situation where civilian deaths are operationally convenient.

Why it fails

"Cannot be distinguished" is the definition of night raids — which is precisely the scenario the questioner asked about. Muhammad's answer was permission, not a prohibition. The exception the jurisprudence carves out swallows the rule wherever military operations are conducted at night, which is historically most of them. The text cannot prevent its own application because it is in the corpus, accurately stated.

Man strikes pregnant wife's belly — judgment is about the fetus, not the woman Women Prophetic Character Moderate Abu Dawud #4570
"[The man] struck his wife's belly..." [leading to the miscarriage case; the judgment focused on the diyah (blood money) owed for the lost fetus, measured as a slave's value]

What the hadith says

A man struck his pregnant wife, causing miscarriage. The Islamic ruling assigned a diyah (blood-money) fine calculated as the price of a slave. The case is preserved as a jurisprudential precedent on fetal compensation.

Why this is a problem

The victim of the assault — the wife — is absent from the ruling. She was beaten; she lost a pregnancy. The judgment concerns the value of the fetus, paid to the family, not to her. Her suffering generates no distinct legal claim. The fetus is valued at the price of a slave, equating the unborn child of a Muslim with the market value of owned property. The domestic violence is treated as a property-damage problem.

Abortion-related and domestic-violence cases in Islamic legal systems still calculate fetal loss using this diyah rule. The ruling is applied classical fiqh, not history.

Why it fails

A case about a woman beaten in the belly that focuses entirely on the lost fetus's slave-price has not recognized the assaulted person. "Broader protections elsewhere" do not appear in this case. The framework treats the assault as property damage because the woman's body is, in the underlying legal structure, subject to the husband's authority. The moral center of the ruling is mis-set by design, not by accident.

Blood money: a woman's life is worth half a man's; a non-Muslim less Women Treatment of Disbelievers Logical Inconsistency Moderate Abu Dawud Book of Diyat
[Classical Islamic ruling, codified from Abu Dawud and parallel collections:] "The diyah of a woman is half the diyah of a man. The diyah of a dhimmi (protected non-Muslim) is one-third or less of a Muslim's."

What the hadith says

Islamic blood-money law assigns different values to different lives. A woman killed is worth half a man in compensation. A Jew or Christian under Islamic protection is worth one-third to one-half of a Muslim. The ratios are built from hadith material and applied in courts for 1,400 years.

Why this is a problem

Iran, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, and other Islamic-law systems still apply diyah in some cases — including traffic fatalities, where non-Muslim women can receive less compensation than Muslim men. The rule directly contradicts Q 5:32's equation of one soul with all humanity: if one soul equals all humanity, soul-value cannot differ by gender or religion. The tradition applies differing rates anyway, revealing that the universalist language of the Quran is overridden by the legal specifics of the hadith.

Slaves are compensated at market price — treating killed persons as damaged property. The category is legally defunct in most jurisdictions, but the underlying logic (human = quantified asset) shaped the entire framework.

Why it fails

Dignity before God that does not translate into equal compensation in a court of law is not legal equality — it is theological consolation. The ratios are applied in courts, not in theology. A legal system that monetizes lives at different rates by religion and sex has not accepted universal human equality regardless of what its cosmology claims.

A pre-pubertal girl's iddah — the Quranic rule that assumes child marriage Women Logical Inconsistency Moderate Q 65:4
[Q 65:4:] "And those who no longer expect menstruation among your women — if you doubt, then their period is three months, and [also for] those who have not menstruated..."

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud operationalizes Q 65:4: the Quran's "those who have not menstruated" clause assigns pre-pubertal girls a three-month iddah after divorce. The rule presupposes that such girls have been divorced — meaning they were first married before puberty.

Why this is a problem

An iddah rule for pre-pubertal divorcées exists because the Quran legislates for their divorce, not because it prohibits their marriage. The verse does not ban child marriage; it codifies its aftermath. Saudi Arabia, Iran, and Yemen's clerical establishments have cited this verse to defend the legal permissibility of marriage before menarche — and the scriptural anchor is solid, not strained.

A girl who has not menstruated cannot meaningfully consent to a marriage. The scriptural framework overrides modern consent standards because it never required consent in the first place.

Why it fails

"Edge-case" readings cannot survive the fact that the verse is actively cited by contemporary Islamic clerics to defend pre-pubertal marriage. If the verse merely addressed irregularities, those citations would be invalid — but they are treated as authoritative. A scripture that legislates the waiting period for divorced prepubertal girls has already granted their marriage and divorce as legal realities. Denying that implication requires abandoning the verse's plain sense.

Abu Dawud documents narrator errors inside the collection itself Logical Inconsistency Moderate Abu Dawud throughout — editorial notes
[Abu Dawud notes:] "Muhammad bin Hassan is unknown, and this Hadith is weak." / "This is a mistake from Ibn 'Uyaynah." / "Its chain is not strong."

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud appends his own commentary throughout the collection, noting when chains are weak, when narrators have made mistakes, and when reports conflict with stronger material. These notes are part of the text, not marginalia.

Why this is a problem

The compiler's doubt is on record for hundreds of hadiths — yet classical fiqh used those same flagged texts anyway. Later jurists frequently ignored Abu Dawud's "weak chain" notes and built legal rulings on material the collector himself distrusted. Formal hadith grading (al-Albani and others) happened centuries after Abu Dawud's death and sometimes overrides his own editorial judgments. This is retroactive certification, not contemporary authentication.

A body of law built on texts whose own compiler admitted uncertainty cannot credibly claim the uniform divine certification that Islamic jurisprudence asserts. Abu Dawud's honesty is a scholarly virtue; it is also evidence that the hadith system's confidence exceeds its stated reliability.

Why it fails

Systematizing the doubt centuries later does not remove the problem that hadiths Abu Dawud flagged as weak were used as legal sources in the interim — and continue to be used where later graders came to different conclusions. A legal system whose foundational texts carry the compiler's own uncertainty flags has not resolved the uncertainty by adding more opinions about it.
